title: The push cut
subtitle: The safe default cut in wood carving
excerpt: The push cut is the first technique every wood carver learns and the one used on most cuts throughout a project. Blade travels away from the body, driven by the forearm. This tutorial covers the grip, the blade angle, what a clean push cut looks and feels like, and what goes wrong.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for cutting directions and grip descriptions in wood-carving instruction.

content.text: The
push cut

is the cut you will make more than any other. It is safe, controllable, and works on green and seasoned wood alike. The blade travels away from the body; the cut ends in open air. If the edge slips, it does not travel toward a hand or body part. Every other cut in wood carving is a variation or supplement to the push cut, and most carvers return to it for the majority of their shaping work on every project.
When to use it

The push cut is the default for most shaping work: taking off bulk to rough out a blank, refining a spoon handle along its length, flattening facets on a whittled piece, and making light finishing passes along the grain. Use it whenever the cut can travel away from the body in a straight or gently curved line and end in open air. The push cut does not suit tight hollowing (use a hook knife for that), or very short, precise chips (use a thumb-pivot or stop cut for those).
What you need
A
sloyd knife
that is sharp enough: it should take a hair off the back of the forearm without pressing. A
strop
charged with compound, close at hand. A piece of green or seasoned wood to practice on; a piece of green sycamore or hazel, 20 to 25 cm long and 3 to 4 cm in diameter, is ideal for a first session.
The technique
Step 1: Grip the work

Hold the workpiece in your non-dominant hand using the four-finger grip: thumb on top of the piece, four fingers underneath, hand wrapped around the wood. The thumb sits on the top face of the blank, pointing in the same direction as the intended cut. The four fingers grip underneath and prevent the piece rotating. The knife hand will work above the work; the cut travels away from your thumb, not across it.
Step 2: Grip the knife

Use the thumb-push grip on the knife: the handle rests in a closed fist, the thumb of the knife hand presses against the spine of the blade just forward of the handle. This thumb-on-spine position is not for pushing the blade; it steadies the blade angle and gives the forearm a reference for the direction of travel. The actual driving force comes from the forearm extension, not from the hand grip.
Step 3: Set the blade angle

Place the blade on the surface of the wood at an angle of 15 to 25 degrees from the long axis of the blank. Steeper angles take a thicker shaving; shallower angles take a finer one. For shaping work, 20 to 25 degrees is practical. For finishing passes, 10 to 15 degrees gives a finer cut. The blade should contact the wood with the first third of the blade length behind the tip, not the tip alone.
Step 4: Drive the cut

Extend the knife arm forward and slightly to the outside, driving the blade along the wood surface away from the body. The motion comes from the elbow, not the wrist. The wrist stays relatively fixed; a loose wrist lets the blade angle change mid-stroke, which produces an uneven cut. The cut should end when the blade exits the wood at the far end of the stroke, into open air.

A clean push cut on green wood produces a thin, slightly glossy shaving that curls away from the blade as it lifts. The cut surface left behind should be smooth and slightly shiny. If the surface is rough or furry, the cut is going against the grain; rotate the piece 180 degrees and try the cut in the opposite direction.
What a correct cut looks like

The shaving comes off as a single continuous curl or a clean strip. The cut surface has no torn fibres. The blade travels smoothly with no catching or stuttering. The forearm does the work; the hand grip is firm but not clenched. If the blade is catching rather than cutting, either the edge is dull (strop immediately) or the cut angle is too steep (reduce to 15 degrees and try again).
Common mistakes
Cutting across the body rather than away from it. The push cut must travel away. If the stroke ends toward the body or hand, reset the workpiece position.
Gripping too tightly. A death-grip on the knife produces a stiff, jerky stroke. The grip should be firm enough to maintain blade angle, not tight enough to whiten the knuckles.

Making one heavy cut instead of several light ones. The push cut is a slicing technique, not a chopping one. Ten light cuts remove more material more controllably than one heavy cut, and the light cut is far less likely to run with the grain past the intended stopping point.
Not stropping often enough. A blunt edge requires force, and force reduces control. Strop every fifteen minutes without waiting for the edge to feel obviously dull.
